posts.text: Thousands protested in Serbia’s northern city of Novi Sad to mark the 2024 deaths of 16 people after a railway station awning collapsed and demand snap general elections https://t.co/kQmDVxZIB5
posts.summary: Serbia's political instability following the deadly railway station collapse and subsequent protests demanding snap elections could create uncertainty that weighs on regional energy markets and investor confidence, though the direct impact on global oil prices is likely minimal given Serbia's limited oil production.

posts.text: 🔊 Bolivia declares a state of emergency after protests shut down economy. For more watch Reuters World News https://t.co/bveKUf8VVx https://t.co/sFU86pBCpw
posts.summary: Bolivia's declaration of a state of emergency amid economy-disrupting protests could potentially disrupt the country's oil production and exports, though Bolivia is a relatively minor oil producer with limited global market impact. Any sustained supply interruption would likely have minimal effect on international crude prices given Bolivia's small share of global output.

posts.text: US disputes Iranian claims about closing Strait of Hormuz as negotiators head to Switzerland https://t.co/pB5n8gy89A https://t.co/pB5n8gy89A
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Summary

The US rejected Iranian threats to close the Strait of Hormuz—a critical chokepoint through which roughly 20-30% of global oil passes—as diplomatic talks proceed in Switzerland, reducing immediate supply disruption risk for crude markets. However, ongoing tensions between the US and Iran continue to create geopolitical uncertainty that could support oil prices if rhetoric escalates.

posts.text: Trump says no toll on Strait of Hormuz unless US imposes one https://t.co/GcW8UTeySa https://t.co/GcW8UTeySa
posts.summary: Trump indicated he would not support tolls on the Strait of Hormuz unless the U.S. implements one itself, signaling a potential shift away from policies that could increase shipping costs through one of the world's most critical oil chokepoints. This stance could ease concerns about supply disruptions and higher energy prices that such tolls might otherwise trigger.

posts.text: 🔊 Strikes in Lebanon just hours after a truce raised questions over how long peace can last. Tune in to Reuters World News for more https://t.co/Sa4FFHaDVq https://t.co/Pq0sT39Ma7
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Summary

Renewed strikes in Lebanon shortly after a ceasefire agreement raise concerns about the sustainability of peace in the region, potentially threatening crude oil supply stability in the Eastern Mediterranean and risking renewed geopolitical risk premiums in energy markets.

posts.text: Iranian negotiators heading to Switzerland. US VP JD Vance expected to travel too later today. https://t.co/LsIUMJNyHj
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Summary

Iranian and US officials are traveling to Switzerland for talks, which could signal potential negotiations that may affect Iranian oil export sanctions and global crude supply dynamics. Any diplomatic progress could increase market uncertainty around future Iranian oil production levels and pricing.

posts.text: Iran says the Strait of Hormuz is now closed. The US says it remains open and that 17 million barrels of oil crossed the waterway today (that’s higher than pre-war, and a lot higher when the bypass pipelines volumes are added). https://t.co/LbkE3Iq9Ya
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Summary

Despite Iran's closure claims, the Strait of Hormuz remains operationally open with oil flows at or above pre-conflict levels, suggesting limited immediate disruption to global energy supplies. The continued high throughput and functioning bypass pipeline capacity indicate current stability in crude transportation, though geopolitical tensions persist.

posts.text: Iran isn’t losing any time re-starting its oil production and fresh exports (📷 via @CopernicusEU Sentinel-2 🛰️ from earlier today, showing multiple super tankers loading at Iran’s Kharg Island terminal) https://t.co/agqZcLzA5H
posts.summary: Iran is rapidly resuming oil production and exports following recent developments, with satellite imagery showing multiple tankers loading at the Kharg Island terminal. This suggests a potential increase in global oil supply that could impact crude prices and international energy markets.

posts.text: Joint Maritime Information Center says oil tankers and other vessels can use the southern (Omani) shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz with “AIS on, radars radiating, running lights on, and normal use of VHF” either at night or day. https://t.co/f7Yyv9SIs5
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Summary

Shipping vessels, including oil tankers, can safely transit the southern Hormuz lane with standard navigation systems operational, suggesting reduced immediate disruption risk to crude oil flows through this critical chokepoint.

posts.text: Geothermal energy has great advantages as a source of zero-carbon dispatchable power, but conventional projects are limited by needing specific geological conditions. Enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) use the technologies of hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling, developed
posts.summary: Enhanced geothermal systems could reduce oil demand for electricity generation and heating by providing dispatchable zero-carbon power without strict geological constraints, potentially lowering global oil consumption in sectors transitioning away from fossil fuels.

posts.text:
MAP OF THE DAY: Iran has published a new chart showing two shipping lanes in Hormuz to the south of the ones that Tehran unveiled weeks ago. The new lanes are to the north of the ones used before the war. The Omani lane remains in operation too. 

Before (left) and now (right) https://t.co/oDIVgmolgN
posts.summary: Iran has established new shipping lanes in the Strait of Hormuz north of previously used routes, creating alternative paths that could potentially reduce disruption risks to global oil transit through the critical chokepoint. The expansion of routing options may provide some mitigation against supply interruptions, though the strategic strait remains vulnerable to regional tensions.

posts.text: Switzerland confirms that the US-Iran (plus Pakistan and Qatar) talks scheduled for today and the weekend had been postponed. The delay comes after strong fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in southern Lebanon.
posts.summary: Talks between the US, Iran, Pakistan, and Qatar have been postponed due to escalating Israel-Hezbollah fighting in southern Lebanon, potentially delaying nuclear negotiations that could affect crude oil supply expectations and geopolitical risk premiums. The delay extends uncertainty over Iranian oil sanctions policy, which typically influences global energy markets.

▶️ https://t.co/liZ32yjpAB #naturalgas https://t.co/kgcZheZ7Ht
posts.summary:
# Natural Gas Storage Report Released

The EIA has released its weekly natural gas storage data, which provides insight into U.S. gas inventory levels and can influence energy commodity pricing, though direct implications for crude oil markets are typically limited unless the data signals broader energy demand shifts.

posts.text:
At the @EnergyCouncil_ this week, where we served as Knowledge Partner, Erik Mielke highlighted a critical shift - capital allocation in energy and metals is being reshaped by volatility, policy uncertainty, and changing demand.

For investors and operators, this means: 
🔷 https://t.co/ZEsl3G6Vvp
posts.summary: Energy and metals capital allocation is being increasingly influenced by volatility, policy uncertainty, and shifting demand patterns, requiring investors and operators to reassess their investment strategies. For the oil market specifically, this suggests capital may be redirected based on geopolitical risks, energy transition policies, and fluctuating demand forecasts rather than traditional supply-demand fundamentals alone.

posts.text: US Navy formally announces the lifting of Iran's ports blockade https://t.co/PEbDVvTb49
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Implications

The lifting of the US Navy's blockade on Iran's ports could significantly increase Iranian crude oil exports to global markets, potentially adding supply pressure and downward pressure on oil prices amid ongoing geopolitical tensions.

posts.text: Saudi Arabia has started moving a cluster of empty  (ballast) VLCC tankers it has kept near India for most of the conflict toward the Gulf of Oman for a potential Strait of Hormuz crossing. Another sign that oil production in the Persian Gulf is/will be coming back. https://t.co/BgrAzxxD46
posts.summary: Saudi Arabia is repositioning empty supertanker vessels toward the Strait of Hormuz, signaling an anticipated increase in Persian Gulf oil production exports. The movement suggests confidence that regional conditions are stabilizing enough to resume or expand crude shipments from the world's largest oil exporter.

posts.text:
And where Kuwait goes, so it would go the rest of the Persian Gulf region. Iraq will face some more difficulties, but for KSA and UAE, it would be a lot easier. 

This is the @Opinion column I wrote June 8 about the re-start of production in the region. 

https://t.co/prPkYTsSwO
posts.summary: Javier Blas discusses how oil production restarts in the Persian Gulf region could proceed at different paces, with Saudi Arabia and the UAE better positioned to resume output quickly compared to Iraq, while Kuwait's recovery trajectory could signal broader regional trends.

posts.text:
MUST READ: Kuwait has started ramping up oil production and plans to reach 2 million b/d within a week (from 0.5m b/d during the war), KPC CEO Sheikh Nawaf Al-Sabah tells @FionamMacDonald in an interview. He says recovery faster than previously thought.
https://t.co/RMethuGdgM
posts.summary: Kuwait is rapidly restoring oil production to 2 million barrels per day, up from 0.5 million during recent conflict, potentially easing global supply concerns faster than anticipated. The accelerated recovery could help stabilize oil prices and reduce market tightness in the near term.

posts.text: Our experts will be attending Intersolar Europe 2026, taking place next week at Messe München in Germany. Across the conference programme, our experts will deliver six presentations and act as a moderator for one, bringing our research, insights and analysis directly to industry
posts.summary: Wood Mackenzie will participate in Intersolar Europe 2026 with six presentations and one moderation role, though the post focuses on solar and renewable energy rather than oil market implications. The company's increased visibility in the renewable energy sector reflects the energy industry's broader transition away from traditional fossil fuels.

posts.text: The longer the Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted, the higher oil, gas and electricity prices will rise. If US/Iran negotiations continue to make progress, and the strait reopens within a few weeks, the outcome will look most like the Quick Peace scenario that WoodMac set out https://t.co/pAANRp57jA
posts.summary: Prolonged disruption of the Strait of Hormuz would drive higher oil and gas prices, while a swift reopening following US-Iran negotiations could align with Wood Mackenzie's "Quick Peace" scenario with more moderate price impacts.

posts.text:
The revenue opportunities of artificial intelligence are immense. But permitting delays and federal policy uncertainty mean the US power sector cannot keep pace with the infrastructure demands of massive data centre development.  
 
The result? Developers are opting for https://t.co/BFqJi3YBJX
posts.summary:
# Summary

US power infrastructure constraints and regulatory delays are driving data center developers toward alternative energy solutions, potentially increasing demand for fossil fuels or renewable energy projects outside traditional grid systems. This could reshape energy demand patterns and create new opportunities in the oil and gas sector depending on which alternative energy sources developers ultimately pursue.

posts.text:
The Iran war has caused the greatest energy supply shock in living memory. Our Horizons special edition lays out three distinct scenarios for the end of the war and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and the implications of these for oil & gas supply, demand and prices. 
 
The https://t.co/bAMWKiK5E3
posts.summary: Wood Mackenzie identifies the Iran conflict as creating an unprecedented energy supply disruption and analyzes three potential scenarios for resolving the crisis and reopening the Strait of Hormuz, with significant implications for global oil and gas markets, supply dynamics, and pricing.

posts.text: The US deregulated power market model faces its greatest test. Data centre growth will depend heavily on natural gas, and the historically high cost of building and operating gas plants presents an enormous challenge in the deregulated markets of PJM and ERCOT. To incentivise new https://t.co/A4bReGrjRS
posts.summary:
# Summary

Rising data center demand is pressuring deregulated US power markets to build natural gas infrastructure, which could increase gas consumption and potentially affect oil prices if gas serves as a substitute for oil in energy generation. The high capital costs of new gas plants may create supply constraints that support energy commodity prices more broadly.
